title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about entrepreneurship and the culture of innovation. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Mythology of the Founder
content.subtitle: An article examining the culture and reality of entrepreneurship.
content.text:
In the popular imagination, the entrepreneur is a heroic figure — a visionary who identifies what the world needs before the world knows it needs it, builds something from nothing, and changes the way we live. Silicon Valley gave this mythology its modern form, with a cast of college dropouts who became billionaires and a vocabulary of disruption that entered everyday language. [31] The image is compelling partly because it contains genuine truth: innovation does require courage, persistence, and a willingness to be wrong repeatedly before getting something right. What it obscures is the role of luck, timing, and structural advantage in determining who gets to be celebrated as a pioneer. [/31]

Research into entrepreneurial success consistently shows that background matters enormously. Those who start businesses are more likely to have parents who ran businesses; they are more likely to have access to financial cushions that allow them to take risks; they are more likely to attend the universities and move in the social circles where the most valuable connections are made. [32] This is not to say that talent and determination are irrelevant — they are clearly not — but that the narrative of the self-made entrepreneur often underplays the infrastructure of privilege that makes risk-taking possible. [/32]

The failure rate among new businesses is another inconvenient element that the entrepreneurial myth tends to pass over. Depending on the sector and the definition used, between sixty and ninety percent of new ventures fail within ten years. [33] Many of those failures involve real hardship — not just for the founder but for employees, suppliers, and investors. The culture of 'failing fast' and 'learning from failure', while genuinely valuable as an attitude, can sometimes trivialise the human cost of businesses that collapse. [/33]

None of this is an argument against entrepreneurship itself, which remains one of the most powerful engines of economic and social change. New businesses create jobs, challenge incumbents, and solve problems that large, risk-averse organisations are ill-equipped to address. [34] The difficulty is in distinguishing between entrepreneurship that genuinely creates value for society and innovation that merely extracts value from existing systems, repackaging services in ways that benefit investors while undermining workers and communities. [/34]

The most celebrated innovations of the digital age have attracted exactly this kind of scrutiny. Ride-hailing platforms, short-term rental services, and food delivery apps have simultaneously created new opportunities and displaced traditional employment. [35] What their founders and backers describe as 'disruption' is sometimes experienced, by those on the receiving end, as a degradation of conditions that previous generations had fought hard to establish. [/35]

A more nuanced view of entrepreneurship would acknowledge both its extraordinary potential and its capacity for harm. [36] The task for policy-makers and citizens alike is not to romanticise or demonise founders, but to create frameworks in which genuine innovation is rewarded while the costs of failure and disruption are distributed more fairly. [/36]

questions.question: What does the author say about the popular image of the entrepreneur?
options: A) It is entirely invented and has no basis in reality.
B) It is accurate but only applies to Silicon Valley companies.
C) It captures some truths but hides the importance of factors beyond talent.
D) It has been deliberately constructed by technology companies.
questions.explanation: The author says the image 'contains genuine truth' about courage and persistence, but 'obscures the role of luck, timing, and structural advantage'.

questions.question: According to the author, research into entrepreneurial success indicates that
options: A) talent and determination are the main predictors of business success.
B) family background and social connections play a significant role.
C) entrepreneurs from wealthy backgrounds are less motivated than others.
D) attending prestigious universities guarantees entrepreneurial success.
questions.explanation: Research shows background 'matters enormously', with family business experience, financial safety nets, and valuable social connections all contributing.

questions.question: The author suggests that the attitude of 'failing fast' is problematic because it
options: A) encourages entrepreneurs to give up too easily.
B) is used to justify avoiding proper business planning.
C) may not adequately consider the suffering caused by business failure.
D) has been proved to be ineffective as a learning strategy.
questions.explanation: The author says this attitude 'can sometimes trivialise the human cost of businesses that collapse', referring to hardship for employees, suppliers, and investors.

questions.question: The author draws a distinction between entrepreneurship that
options: A) operates in digital versus traditional industries.
B) is backed by investors versus self-funded ventures.
C) genuinely benefits society and that which primarily benefits investors.
D) is started by individuals versus entrepreneurship within large companies.
questions.explanation: The author distinguishes between innovation that 'creates value for society' and innovation that 'merely extracts value from existing systems' benefiting investors at the expense of workers.

questions.question: When the author refers to what workers experience as 'a degradation of conditions', the implication is that
options: A) digital platforms have created entirely new types of jobs.
B) disruption benefits consumers but may harm workers' rights.
C) technology companies refuse to negotiate with trade unions.
D) most workers prefer traditional employment to platform work.
questions.explanation: The author contrasts the founders' language of 'disruption' with what workers experience — the loss of hard-won conditions — suggesting consumers and investors gain while workers lose.

questions.question: The author concludes that the best approach to entrepreneurship is to
options: A) reduce regulation so that innovative companies can operate freely.
B) hold founders personally responsible for any negative social effects.
C) recognise both its benefits and its potential harms, and regulate accordingly.
D) focus policy support only on companies that create large numbers of jobs.
questions.explanation: The author calls for acknowledging 'both its extraordinary potential and its capacity for harm' and creating frameworks that reward innovation while distributing costs fairly.
